content:
3 min read
Just now

--

AI can summarize your notes, but it cannot decide which sources deserve your trust. That part still needs a system.
The easiest way to misuse AI at work is to treat research like a pile of links.
You save an article. You copy a paragraph. You screenshot a chart. You ask a model to summarize everything later. For a while, it feels efficient. Then you try to write a proposal, article, or decision memo and realize the notes do not answer the most important question: why did this source matter?
That is the problem a practical research notebook should solve.
I would keep the system simple. Every research note needs four fields:
1. Claim.
2. Source.
3. Evidence.
4. Use.
The claim is the idea you are trying to support or test. Not “AI tools.” Not “remote work.” A real claim sounds like: “Meeting summaries are more useful when they capture decisions, owners, deadlines, and open questions.”
The source is where the information came from. Save the title, link, author or organization when available, and the date you accessed it. This is not busywork. It prevents the slow drift where a quote becomes “something I read somewhere.”
The evidence is what the source actually says. This should be short and specific. Do not paste half the page into your notes. Write the relevant point in your own words, then keep a link back to the original.
The use is where the note will go. Is it for a client update? A Medium article? A product decision? A personal workflow test? If you cannot name the use, the note may not be worth saving.
This structure is especially useful when AI is involved because it gives the model better boundaries.
Instead of asking, “Summarize these links,” you can ask, “Group these notes by claim, flag weak evidence, and tell me which sources need manual verification before publication.”
That is a better task. It keeps the human responsible for trust and uses AI for organization.
Here is a small example.
Claim: Remote workers lose time when meeting decisions are not captured in a reusable format.
Source: internal meeting notes from a fictional client project.
Evidence: three action items were repeated in two later calls because nobody had recorded owners and deadlines.
Use: article section about meeting capture.
That note is not fancy, but it is useful. It can become a paragraph, a checklist item, or a reminder to change the meeting template.
The notebook should also have a “verify before publishing” tag. Any note that includes platform rules, product features, pricing, legal requirements, health claims, tax details, financial advice, or current statistics should be marked for manual fact-checking.
This is where many AI-assisted writers get sloppy. They ask for a confident draft before the evidence is stable. The result sounds polished but rests on weak material.
A better workflow is:
- collect notes;
- label the claim;
- record the source;
- summarize the evidence;
- decide the use;
- verify risky facts;
- draft only after the source trail is clear.
The goal is not to build an academic database. The goal is to make your thinking easier to inspect.
If a note cannot explain its claim, source, evidence, and use, it is probably not research yet. It is just saved material.
AI makes it easier to process information. That raises the standard for what we choose to trust.
Disclosure: This article was prepared with AI-assisted drafting and editorial support, then manually reviewed for clarity, specificity, and platform-safety before publication. It is practical workflow commentary, not professional, legal, tax, financial, medical, or investment advice.
